title: Frequent mouth ulcers
excerpt: Those small white ulcers that flare on the cheek and tongue, again and again, are what Ayurveda calls mukhapaka. The classical texts read them as heat in the mouth, often fed by a tired gut, and they point to cooling, gentle care.
at_a_glance: Recurring mouth ulcers map to mukhapaka, read in Ayurveda as heat (pitta) in the mouth, often fed by a tired gut.
Cut chilli, sour pickles and fried food for a week or two, drink more water, and lean on cooling, soft, ripe foods.
See a doctor for any ulcer that lasts beyond two to three weeks, is hard-edged, bleeds, or comes with a lump, fever or weight loss.

body.heading: the small sore that keeps coming back
paragraphs: You bite into something hot, your tongue catches it, and the next morning there it is: a tiny white crater on the inside of your cheek or under your tongue, sore enough that even tea stings. For some people it heals in a few days and stays away. For others it shows up every few weeks, and that pattern of coming back is the part that wears you down.

Ayurveda has a plain name for this: mukhapaka, which simply means a cooking or inflaming of the mouth. The classical surgical text describes a whole family of mouth conditions (mukharoga) and groups the raw, burning ulcers among them (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana, chapter on mukharoga). The word paka, cooking, is the clue: the lining of the mouth feels as if it has been scalded from the inside.
body.pullQuote: the recurring ulcer is often less about the mouth and more about a tired, overheated digestive fire

body.heading: why Ayurveda reads it as heat
paragraphs: The texts tie these ulcers most often to pitta, the principle of heat and sharpness in the body. When pitta runs high, it surfaces in the soft, moist places first, and the mouth is one of them. Madhava Nidana, the classical manual of diagnosis, places mukhapaka among the mouth disorders and links the spreading, burning, red-and-white sores to aggravated pitta (Madhava Nidana, section on mukharoga). Bhavaprakasha, in its chapter on mouth diseases, describes the same picture of a mouth that burns and ulcerates when heat dominates (Bhavaprakasha, Madhyama Khanda, mukharoga).

There is a second thread the granthas keep returning to, and it matters for the people who get ulcers again and again: the state of the gut. Ayurveda treats digestion, agni, as the root of how clean or how heated the rest of the body runs. When digestion is weak or irritated, by too much chilli and sour and fried food, by skipped and irregular meals, by too little water, the body carries more internal heat upward, and the mouth is where it shows. So the recurring ulcer is often less about the mouth and more about a tired, overheated digestive fire (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, on agni and the formation of disease). Sharp, salty, sour and very hot foods are named across the texts as things that inflame pitta, which is exactly why the people who eat that way most tend to flare most.

body.heading: gentle everyday habits that cool the mouth
paragraphs: The Ayurvedic approach here is unglamorous and mostly about taking the heat down and letting the gut settle. Drink more plain water through the day, since a dry, dehydrated mouth bruises and ulcerates more easily. Lean on cooling, sweet and slightly bitter foods, ripe and soft, and ease off the chilli, the very sour pickles, the deep-fried and the bitingly hot for a week or two while things heal.

For the sore itself, the texts favour cooling rinses and soothing local care of the mouth (Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsasthana, on mukharoga). A simple warm-not-hot rinse, swishing a little water mixed with a pinch of turmeric and salt, or rinsing with cooled boiled water in which coriander has been steeped, follows that same spirit of keeping the mouth clean and cool. Coriander and fennel are both described in the Nighantu tradition as cooling and pitta-pacifying, which is why people reach for a cool coriander or fennel water when the mouth is on fire (Bhavaprakasha Nighantu). Eat slowly so you stop biting the same spot, and give the area a few quiet days rather than poking at it.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: Most mouth ulcers are harmless and settle on their own in a week or two. But see a doctor or a qualified vaidya if an ulcer lasts longer than two to three weeks without healing, keeps coming back in large crops, is unusually large or deep, or is so painful you cannot eat or drink. Get checked promptly for any ulcer with a hard or raised edge, a patch that bleeds easily, a lump in the mouth or neck, fever, or weight loss, since a sore that simply will not heal needs a proper look. This article is general information drawn from the classical texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care.

try_this_today.title: cooling coriander rinse
try_this_today.description: Steep a spoon of coriander seeds in a cup of hot water, let it cool fully, then swish it gently around the mouth a couple of times a day. Coriander is described as cooling and pitta-pacifying in the Nighantu tradition.

try_this_today.title: turmeric-salt swish
try_this_today.description: Add a pinch of turmeric and a pinch of salt to a small cup of warm (not hot) water and rinse gently. This keeps the sore clean and follows the texts' emphasis on cooling, soothing local mouth care.

try_this_today.title: ease the plate for two weeks
try_this_today.description: Step back from chilli, very sour pickles, and deep-fried and bitingly hot food while the ulcer heals. These are exactly the sharp, sour, hot tastes the granthas name as pitta-inflaming.

try_this_today.title: keep the mouth wet
try_this_today.description: Sip plain water through the day. A dry mouth bruises and ulcerates more easily, and steady hydration is one of the simplest ways to take internal heat down.

ingredients.name: Coriander
ingredients.description: Described in the Nighantu tradition as cooling and pitta-pacifying; a cooled coriander rinse soothes a burning mouth.

ingredients.name: Fennel
ingredients.description: A gentle cooling seed; fennel water is a common everyday choice when the mouth and gut feel overheated.

words_to_know.definition: Literally a cooking or inflaming of the mouth; the Ayurvedic name for raw, burning mouth ulcers.
The classical category of mouth and oral diseases, of which mukhapaka is one.
The bodily principle of heat and sharpness; when high, it tends to surface as burning and ulceration.
The digestive fire; its strength governs how clean or how overheated the rest of the body runs.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana and Chikitsasthana, chapters on mukharoga (mouth diseases) · Madhava Nidana, Section on mukharoga (diagnosis of mouth disorders) · Bhavaprakasha, Madhyama Khanda, chapter on mukharoga; and Bhavaprakasha Nighantu on dhanyaka (coriander) as a cooling, pitta-pacifying herb · Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, on agni and digestion in the formation of disease
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
